interactions.summary: Jiang continues the civil-war scenario with urban revolt, cycles of strongman consolidation, and a domestic distraction that lets Middle East eschatology proceed.
The host briefly interjects to keep the thread moving.
Jiang adds the Catholic Church as a hidden major player in the coming American realignment and disintegration.
The host asks whether the deeper fault line is really left versus right, and Jiang says the visible partisan split hides a struggle between establishment elites and emerging elites.

claims.claim: Jiang predicts open revolt in cities such as New York and Boston, possible secessionist pressure from California, and localized sieges rather than nationwide uniform warfare.
Jiang compares the coming American disorder to recurrent Roman civil wars, in which consolidation alternates with renewed conflict.
Jiang says American internal expansion and civil conflict will distract the country long enough for Middle East eschatological plans to unfold.
Jiang says the Catholic Church is a more important hidden actor in American politics than most people realize and may either fold America in or help break it apart.
Jiang says the deeper American conflict is between establishment interests and emerging elite interests rather than simply left versus right.

signature_moments.moment: America is pictured less as 1861 than as a Roman machine of recurring civil war and consolidation.
signature_moments.source_phrase: No different from what happened during Roman times.
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It shifts Jiang's model from national fracture to imperial cyclical violence.
